chapter: JEF P104 – A Question of Trust
title: A Question of Trust – Passage Based Question Set
instruction: Read each passage carefully and answer the questions. Answers must be taken only from the given paragraph.

passages.paragraph: Horace Danby was a successful locksmith who was well respected in his town. He appeared to be an honest and respectable citizen. However, behind this respectable image, he secretly stole once every year to collect money for buying rare and expensive books.
questions: Who was Horace Danby by profession?
How was Horace viewed by the people in his town?
How often did Horace steal?
What was the purpose of Horace’s thefts?
What contrast is shown in Horace’s character?

passages.paragraph: Horace Danby loved books passionately and was ready to break the law for them. He believed that stealing from the rich once a year did not make him a real criminal. This belief helped him justify his dishonest actions.
questions: What did Horace Danby love passionately?
For what reason was Horace ready to break the law?
Whom did Horace steal from?
How did Horace view himself as a criminal?
What helped Horace justify his actions?

passages.paragraph: Horace planned his robberies very carefully. He studied the house, observed the servants’ routine, and examined the wiring. His careful planning gave him confidence that he would not be caught.
questions: How did Horace plan his robberies?
What did Horace study before the robbery?
Why did Horace observe the servants?
What did Horace examine in the house?
What effect did planning have on Horace?

passages.paragraph: The robbery was planned at Shotover Grange when the servants had gone out. The quiet atmosphere and empty house made Horace feel safe and confident about carrying out his plan.
questions: Where was the robbery planned?
When did Horace choose to commit the robbery?
What made the house quiet?
How did the atmosphere affect Horace?
Why did Horace feel confident?

passages.paragraph: Horace suffered from hay fever, which made him sneeze frequently. During the robbery, his sneezing made him uncomfortable and revealed his human weakness.
questions: What illness did Horace suffer from?
What problem did this illness cause?
When did Horace experience discomfort?
What did sneezing reveal about Horace?
How is Horace described in this situation?

passages.paragraph: Inside the house, Horace met a young lady who behaved calmly and confidently. She pretended to be the owner’s wife and spoke in a commanding tone, which made Horace believe her.
questions: Whom did Horace meet inside the house?
How did the young lady behave?
What did the young lady pretend to be?
How did she speak to Horace?
Why did Horace believe her?

passages.paragraph: The young lady claimed that she had forgotten the safe’s combination. She warned Horace that she would call the police if he did not help her, which frightened him.
questions: What problem did the young lady claim to have?
What did she say she had forgotten?
What threat did she give Horace?
How did the threat affect Horace?
Why did Horace agree to help her?

passages.paragraph: To light the young lady’s cigarette, Horace removed his gloves. This careless act caused him to leave fingerprints on the safe, which later became important evidence.
questions: Why did Horace remove his gloves?
What careless act did Horace commit?
What did Horace leave on the safe?
Why were the fingerprints important?
How did this act affect Horace later?

passages.paragraph: The police later found Horace’s fingerprints on the safe at Shotover Grange. This clear evidence led to his arrest and imprisonment.
questions: What did the police find on the safe?
Where were the fingerprints found?
What did the fingerprints prove?
What action did the police take?
What was the result of this evidence?

passages.paragraph: In prison, Horace worked as an assistant librarian. Though he was surrounded by books, he lost his freedom. This ending highlights the irony of his situation.
questions: What work did Horace do in prison?
What surrounded Horace in prison?
What did Horace lose despite being with books?
What does the ending highlight?
Why is Horace’s situation ironic?
